Welcome everybody. We've got a very special day today. We've got a lot of you in here today. That's great to see. Love to see your faces. All right. Well, because we are pressed for time here and I want you all to get your questions in. Greetings. I am Eileen Cartwright. I will be today's moderator. Dr. Chaldini will be discussing some common mistakes that we can make when interpreting and applying the principles. As we say, the science can seem deceptively simple until you really start studying the research and diving deeper, which is kind of where all of you are at right now. He's going to talk for about 20 minutes and then we'll open up the floor to your questions. As always, before we begin, please have your microphones muted unless you're called on. Feel free to use the chat area to send your questions. You can also raise your hand by using the reactions button at the bottom. So to keep us on time, take it away, Bob. Well, thank you, Ily. I'm very happy to be with everyone this morning here in Arizona, maybe later in the day elsewhere. But I think he's right. I want to talk about common mistakes that often trip us up in the use of the principles of influence, either by getting the category wrong that we think we're using, or by failing to optimize the force that the that the principles can provide to move people in our direction. So some of these that I'll talk about you've heard before you've heard me talk about before if you've seen me in some of my interviews. But there are some twists that recent research suggests can improve on rectifying the mistakes we might make. So let's begin right at the top of the of the list of principles that I talk about and I write about the principle of reciprocity. Of course, that says people say yes to those they owe they so if we go first and provide gifts, favors services benefits advantages, they will feel gratified they'll feel a sense of gratitude. They'll want to give back they'll also feel a sense of obligation. The mistake that I think a lot of people make is when they give at information at the to a client or to a prospect. They often do it in terms of a white paper or an article or a set of data that promotes their interests. They say these are the reasons why it would be best to move in our direction. That's no longer a gift. It's no longer a service. It's no longer anything except a sales device at that point in the minds of the people that we're providing this beneficial information for. So I think we need to separate the gifting process from away from the sales process the gifting process occurs first and it involves getting people to see us as generous and worthy partners who can provide good information for us in for us in a general way. So, for example, giving people information about their industry that you've come across. Maybe you're traveling on a plane or you see you're at a conference and you see something about a customer's industry that would be very, very valuable for them to know. That would be worth just sending them a note saying this is what I learned, or you see an article that says top five mistakes to avoid in this industry or in this process or the top five things to remember when you are dealing with customers in general, their customers, right. So that is just a broad form of information that they have available to them. They don't get to see it as designed to further your interest and therefore undercut its intent. So that would be one thing. The other thing about the reciprocity rule that I think I may have said often enough in interviews that you've heard is a mistake that I used to make all the time. And that is after I had gone out of my way to do something above and beyond the call of duty for someone else. And they thank me and say, well, I really appreciate that. I would wave it away. I would say, oh, it was no big deal. No problem. Don't think a thing of it. All right, just part of the job is what it's what I do, right. That's a big mistake because you earned that gratitude. You earned that by going out of your way. You don't want to dismiss it. You don't want to diminish it. You don't want to deny that you did it. You want to put it on the map so that it's there because the world works better when those who give receive in return. All right. If you stop receiving in return, you're going to be less likely to give in the first place. So the society makes sure that there's an obligation to give back to those who have first given to us. Well, we don't want to get in the way of that obligation of that sense of gratitude, which makes for partnerships, makes for existing relationships when people give and return valuable information or services or even items of value. Okay. So what do you say then. I have always said, say this, if it's somebody inside your organization, say, of course, I was glad to do it. It's what we do for one another here. With that, you've established the norm for the exchange. It isn't just one way. It's a mutual interaction where this is what we do for one another. And that's, you've got it on the map now. And so when you need something, people are likely to want to give back in return because that's what we do for one another here. Okay, what if it's somebody outside of your organization? What could you say then, after they've expressed their appreciation. You could say, of course, I was glad to do it. And I know that if the situation were ever reversed, you do the same for me. Once again, you put it on the map, you don't deny that it occurred that there's any obligation or gratitude associated with it. It was, you did go beyond above and beyond. You're entitled to what society says makes for healthy interactions you give that you receive after you've given. Now, I'm going to add a new twist to those two recommendations. Instead of just saying, of course, it's what we do for one another here. Pause and say, right. And instead of saying, of course, I know if the situation were ever reversed, you do the same for me. Pause and say, right. They will say, right. Yes, of course. And now what have you done? Who knows what principle have you engaged? Yeah. Who can speak? Consistency. Consistency. You've had them just make an uncoerced, active commitment to helping you. All right. Oh, and one more mistake that people will make when they use that second response that I mentioned. Of course, I know if the situation were ever reversed, you do the same for me. Right. They say, I know if the situation had been reversed, you would have done the same for me. That's a mistake. That situation will never occur. It's in the past. Don't go back there to someplace that will never appear. You say, if the situation were to be reversed, I know you would do the same for me. Right. Okay, so that's a couple of mistakes that we can retrofit and eliminate in the reciprocity principle. Here's another one. One that we've talked about as well. And that is inside the unity principle where we want people to feel a sense of we-ness with us, a sense of partnership with us. And what we've suggested is, if you already have an ongoing relationship and a long-term relationship, you would be a fool not to mention that at the outset of your interaction before you make an ask. That is, you know, we've been working together for a long time. We've been customers and partners and so on. You say that and suddenly you've brought to mind the concept of we-ness, of unity. And from that standpoint, people think differently about the next thing that you have to say. So that's one thing. But what if you don't have a long-term relationship? Well, the answer is you generate exchanges that create a sense of partnership, that create a sense of we-ness. And the one that we've talked about as a one-word change is, when you, let's say you've got a colleague who you want to come on board with an idea that you have for an initiative, that you would like to push up the line inside your organization, or you have an idea for a new product or service that you would like to get support for. And you ask that person, you show them a blueprint or a summary of your idea, and you ask for their opinion on it. Asking for partnership, getting people to agree with you is exactly the right thing to do. To do it by asking for an opinion is a mistake. When you ask for an opinion, you get a critic. You get someone who does the opposite of unitizing with you, who steps back from you and goes into themselves, and they introspect about the pros and cons and criticisms that they could develop for this idea of yours. If you change one word, and the research is very clear on this, if you change one word and ask for their advice rather than opinion, you get a partner in the process. Someone who is a collaborator with you, someone who is working with you to move this idea forward. And the research, as I suggested, shows that they will be, first of all, more favorable to your idea if you change the word from opinion to advice. Secondly, the same thing applies to asking for feedback. It's a mistake. It's the same mistake. People step away from you instead of toward you. And then finally, when you ask for an opinion, you get better input. It's theirs. So they try harder and they work harder to give you the best possible advice on the project. Okay, now here's the newest research. When you ask for advice, don't do it in a public, multi-person session, like in a meeting, because observers will think less of you if you're always asking for advice from everybody. They'll think that you don't have the intellectual wherewithal to think this through on yourself. That flips if you ask it in private of a single person, and that person is the one who thinks better of your intelligence. Benjamin Franklin said this in a quote. He said, There's a firing of them, which they love to believe. Okay, so that's a couple. There's one now that is often a mistake. I know many of you are coaches, but it's not just when in a situation, some of you are managers, team leaders, and so on. If you have a goal for the person or the people that you're working with and trying to lead them to that goal, and they do a good job of it, they get halfway there in a timely and effective way, they deserve a compliment. They deserve to be reinforced for the movement that they've had in the correct direction. But what the mistake is that we typically make is to congratulate them on their progress toward the goal. Here's what happens when you congratulate people and focus them on their progress. They take a step back and say, Good, I've done all this already. It's time for some me time. They take their foot off the pedal and they stop moving in a timely way because of all the progress. Think what you've done when you've asked them or you've told them to congratulate them on their progress. That means you've told them to look around behind them to look back at what they've already done, rather than forward to the space that still needs to be traversed to the goal. But you can do it with one word. You congratulate them on their commitment to the goal. The very same evidence of how well they've moved when it's described as progress causes them to relax and look backward. If you describe it as your commitment to the goal, you cause them to look forward to achieving the goal. The same evidence. It's the evidence of how well they've done so far. You can say to them, This is my evidence of how committed you are to this goal. Now you've got the commitment principle working again in your favor. Instead of something like I deserve a break. Let me get on Facebook for a while and check in on my network. No. Commitment and they continue to pedal. They keep their foot on the of a bike or the foot on the pedal of the car. They go further because you've reminded them you've reintegrated the idea of a commitment, their desire to reach that goal. Okay, I'm going to do one more. And we'll come back maybe another time to do some of the others. Here's one I just learned about. It has to do with the scarcity principle. You know that people want more of what they can have less of, and we've always taught. It's not enough when you have something unique or rare or dwindling in availability to tell them, not just what they will gain. If they move in your direction of your offering, but what they will forgo. What they will lose, what they will miss, because as Daniel Kahneman showed us in his prospect theory, people are twice as motivated to avoid losing a unit of value as gaining that same thing twice as motivated. So you have to tell them what that you, you wouldn't want them the way you don't say it in a way that threatens them. You just I wouldn't want to see you lose this opportunity or this feature. That's part of our upgraded version whatever the the the unavailable thing might be if they move away. So, okay. Now, there's a guy who wrote to me after hearing one of my lectures, and he said he could he's in a country where there's a lot of shoreline a lot of beaches. And in the summer vendors go up and down the beach is selling food selling little items ice cream in is a popular one in the summer. And so what the what the vendors would do is they come, they walk down a beach and they say I'm coming with ice cream I'm coming with my ice cream. And ring a little bell. Good, they got a good response to that. Because everybody likes ice cream. But one of them looked at the power of loss aversion. And when he walks through the group, he says, I'm leaving. I'm leaving with my ice cream. I'm leaving. And what has happened according to this correspondent who wrote to me is now everybody is doing that all the ice cream vendors because it works so much better. All of a sudden people jump at the chance to get the ice cream that's leaving. It's being lost. So, again, the bigger mistake to avoid, because you think that you don't want to say anything negative to people about what they will lose or is a no, that's wrong. If you give people honest information about what they stand to miss, they jump at the chance to avoid losing that thing of them. Okay, so I think we're probably at the point where I suggested that I can stop speaking in this way and turn to a more interactive section of our time today. Yes, Bob, we do have quite a lot of hands raised. I'm impressed every single time. I think I've heard the story. You add some little nuance that changes my whole outlook on it. Well, this is the thing. I'm like a shark in the water when it comes to research on social influence. I'm reading all the journals. I'm reading all the new books on the topic. I'm reading what other people have found or learned about the influence process that I can use to enhance and tweak what we've already been saying. Well, I find it valuable. I'm sure everybody does. But let's just dive into some questions. Robert has had his hand up first. Robert Smith, do you want to unmute yourself? Hi, how are you, Dr? I'm well, Robert. Thank you. A couple of quick questions. One is, I'm impressed with the yellow backgrounds and all the videos and on Cheldini.com. So the first part is why the yellow backgrounds and the second part is why didn't you take that to influence at work.com? Okay, well, the yellow backgrounds are to generate attention that yellow grabs your attention more than any other color. So we're new. We need attention. Influence at work has been around for 25 years. And we can rely on other kinds of strategies to generate focus, I think, than color. Perfect. Thank you very much. That answers all that. Thank you. By the way, feel welcome to ask me questions about the material I just talked about, as well as broader questions, just like Robert did in his question. Yeah. Yeah, I think some people have had some questions saved up. So let's go to Tatiana. How are you, Tatiana? Hello, everybody. Hello, Dr. Cheldini. This is my first session Q&A with you. And it actually helped me a lot to solve very difficult questions in my personal and professional life. But something very important happened to me recently when I joined your institute. And actually what happened to me after I watched all your videos, I really, I recognized my mission. And it's such a relief inside, because thanks to your emphasis on ethics, I understood that my mission as a trainer on global communication on truth evaluation is to show as many people as possible that honesty is the key to prosperity in personal and professional life. And thanks to you, this understanding, it gave me so much energy. And now I want to spread this knowledge as much as possible. And here comes my first influence challenge. I want to scale my business and I want to attract in my company the best talents. And I need your advice how to do this, how to motivate the best talents to come to my company, which is not well known brand yet. Thank you. Good to see you again, Tatjana. Tell me how long have you been in this particular company, the one that you've been in for six years. It's my own company. And I work as a trainer on global communication for six years. It actually I studied with Poletman, with John Avara, and but I am alone. And now I really want to scale your motivation. So six years. So, yeah, I'm going to ask you to use a principle of social proof that comes from being successful over six years. Right. When you started, right, how many, how many customers did how many clients did you have? One. And then the next year, how many? Oh, the next year. So it was more, it was like, maybe, let's say seven. Yeah. Now I bet you could answer the same way for each of the, even though it may, you'd like it to be larger, right? Yes. The fact is not that it may not be the largest. It's that it's been progressively growing. That it's a trend. And what research shows. So if you say to somebody, so, let's say you have a, this is a little different from your situation, but if you said, we have a new upgrade or a new addition to what we offer. 30% of our clients have chosen it. That's a mistake because it means 70% have not, right, by the suit. But if you say, when we started it a year ago, it was 15%. Three months later, it was 20%. And then another six months, and it was 25. And today it's 30. It's the same 30, right? But is the 30 at the end of a trend is dramatically more persuasive at causing people to think that what you have is worthwhile because more and more people are rising to that opportunity. Right. So a mistake that we often make is that we fail to show people the trend in our direction. Even if it isn't the largest selling or has the greatest, you know, market share, the trend. Because my team has done research on that. The reason is people don't think 30 is the end. They project it. You project the trend into the future. Right. And they think that soon it will be much larger. And you've got, you've got future social proof in your working in your behalf now, then negative social proof at 30%. So that's what I would say. Use the fact that you have been moving. That's going to attract the elite. And to the extent that you can categorize the extent to which elite people have been joining. That's even better. Because now you've got comparable other people who are like them joining this. This trend to future success. This skyrocket trend. Yeah, no, and here's what I'm going to say. If you give them 30%. That's a statistic. If you give the if you say. When we started, we, or we had one or set, let's say seven. And now we have 30. Okay. That's a change. But we all know that changes don't stay stable. Changes can level off to stay at 30, or they can drop back down. But a trend only goes up in people's minds. Not true. But the psychology is if you see a regular trend. You assume that it will continue in that direction. Right. So you need three data points, not just the statistic, not just a change. You need a trend. Three. Okay, I can even show six and the show. That's right. Right. Do it right in the middle. Right. Zero to halfway to right six. Yeah. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you. Tatiana. Let's go to a question from the chat. Sharon McCarthy, if you want to unmute yourself. Great. Thanks. Thanks, Dr. Chaldeany. I really appreciate you answering this question. So in this new era of AI, some of the professional service businesses are going to be using AI. And they're going to have a hard time, or at least I hypothesize, about maintaining their average price because so much of their value has been in perceived level of effort. And so I'd love to get your thoughts on how they can maintain that same value in this era of AI when people know that AI API is 30 bucks. It costs 20 bucks a month to use it. So just would love your thoughts on how do you maintain that perceived value. This is a good example of a larger question that I see confronting us. As we use more AI, as we use more technology in one sort or another, it separates us from one another. Technology, we now shop apart from others. We now entertain ourselves by clicking on Google or YouTube or Netflix apart from others. We work individually at a computer. And the things that cause people to say yes to us most directly are human interactions. And the trend is to separate ourselves. And this would be a good example. I mean, why should I pay for that? This machine is generating these numbers and I don't have any contact with them. I don't have any positive feelings toward them. I don't have any connection to them. Why would I trust somebody that I don't have any interactions with. So I've just finished writing a PowerPoint program to deal with this. And an example is what can we do? We're not going to hold back technology. That's naive. It's just too immediate. It's too scalable. It's too efficient. We're not going to hold back technology. Here's what we have to do. We have to imbue personalism into the technology. We have to find a way to build human connection into the machine-generated interactions we have with people. How do you do that? Well, there's a whole set of things you can do. And I'm going to be talking about this. We're going to have an event in May where people can come. We'll have a two-day conference here in Arizona. And this is one of the presentations I'm going to make. So here's an example. And it's one we've talked about before. It's another one of these small bigs. There was a study of 6,700 online commercial sites. And they looked at what were the factors that if they were included in that site versus removed from it, an A-B test, increased conversions, increased purchases. And they included all kinds of things like economic factors, for example. Is there free shipping? They included technological wizardry. Is there a search function on the site so you can go all around and find with ease what you want? Even some sales-related things. Like, was there a call to action line at the end of the presentation? None of those made any difference. But the principles of influence that we've been talking about. Reciprocity, scarcity, social proof authority. They were at the top. But you know, what was also at the top? Liking. Well, how do you get liking on a website where you don't know people? That you've never had any dealings with them? It was to have a welcoming statement on the landing page of your site. Welcome to our site. We're so glad that you're here. That significantly increased conversions because it brought a human dimension into the technology. I should put that on my website when I heard you. Great. Well, there are others like that. There are others like that. For example, when you welcome them, do it in a handwritten font. Not a mechanical font. Because if there's a handwritten message, they've done this even with product packaging, where the name of the product is written in script, versus mechanical block letters. And you've got a sense of more, what these researchers call person presence, that there's a person there. We need people are going to crave that more and more and more. The more technology separates us. So the way to beat this is to find ways to install commentary or techniques that tell people that there's a person here, a person who likes you, a person who approves of you, a person who welcomes you. Wouldn't it be true? We're always invested, just as Tatiana was saying, we're invested in the ethics of this. Isn't it true that you would welcome somebody who came into your site? You would be favorable to that person. We just don't show it. Anyway, I'm working on a whole series of things that we can do to produce that. Produce the sense of connection and weaness that we can put into our websites. So, any of our communications. So, for example, when you send an email, and you put your signature at the end, put it in handwriting, a handwriting script, a handwriting font. It's, you know, it says Bob or it says Sharon, not print it out. It just implies there's a person here and people are looking for that. Isn't it true that Sharon is really there? The person, we just have to make sure we reveal the humanity of what we are offering and who we are as a person. I so agree with that. That is fantastic. And I really look forward to seeing that content. Hopefully it's something I can attend because I think the future is human. And I think the challenge will be how do we introduce humanity into our technology at scale. So I love it. That's, that's a bullseye observation. Yeah. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you so much. Okay, let's go right away to Karthik. Did you want to unmute yourself? We can't hear you Karthik. Yes, there you go. Thank you Kelly and hello Dr. Bob. So happy to be here with you on learning from you. So recently I used one of the principles at my work where I was, you know, there was a very dire situation in my team where we were losing people because of budget issues and all and we had to secure budget from our existing clients, you know. So, at the same situation, one of our clients, they had a challenge of, you know, putting in $125,000 every year into a product. And they were also running out of the budget. You know, so I was able to, I just, I'm just curious to know about this, that when I asked them, in fact, when the conversation started, he challenged me saying, Karthik, do you even think that it's important that we save money in our organization? That's how the conversation started with them. And towards the end, I asked him, look, we also need budget and you are also losing money on this product. So are you able to invest $46,000 into our team so that we can support you and also, you know, you can be our partner in this. And finally, he agreed and we got budget and we're working together right now. So I'm just curious to know what's your immediate thought on that and what influence principles work here. Yeah, I think two things were true. And I think I heard you say both of them. One was what I suggested at the outset, that you mentioned that you had a long term relationship with them. That's a unity approach that we're together on this. Now let's work together. And we have the same problem. We can solve your problem if you can help solve ours. So now, and you said to them, I see that you have a problem with budget, am I right? Yes. And they said yes. Okay, which allows you to say, well, you've committed yourself to finding ways to reduce this problem. Here's a way to do it. Allow us to staff our teams to give you a better outcome with this particular product that you have. So they committed themselves to wanting to solve it and you gave them a way to solve it. And that's that aligned with their commitment to move forward. And the fact that you had a long term relationship suggests that you already had a partnership that would allow such a solution to be to be easy to accomplish, given what we know about each other. Great. Great example. And by the way, I would love it if we taught each other in these Q&A series, people talk about situations that they had that they were able to resolve by understanding the best way to move forward. So, again, with social proof, I don't want to suggest that I'm the only one who has the answers. Your colleagues, your contemporaries, you know, they will know exactly the situations that you're facing very often, and they can provide some information. So feel welcome to raise your hand and speak to any of those that are top of mind. All right, fantastic. Thank you, Karthik. Let's move on to Christian. Can you hear me? Yes. Thank you. Doctor, I show my age a little bit. I might be pretty far back. Back in the night, I was at Augustana College in Rock Island, Illinois in 1988 I was in a friend's dorm room, and he had just finished reading this book that he was assigned in his sociology class. I thumbed through it. I said, hey, it looks interesting. Turns out this is the only book I ever read in college that I wasn't assigned. So thank you for that. By digress. So in the liking principle, one of our amplifiers is getting them to like us first. Or excuse me, the other way around, us liking them first. So we need to like them first. In a retail situation, maybe a high ticket like car sales, like maybe jewelry or fine clothes, sometimes customers come in at a moment's notice. You turn around and they're there. Maybe one of them is wearing something, a shirt that is socially, you're not in 100% agree with it. You take a fence to it. And so help us out in a situation like that, finding something to like in that situation. Yeah, finding something to like. That's the key. So don't focus on the shirt. You know, say something else about them that you've noticed that you find admirable or commendable about them. That would be the way to do it. Be a detective of things that are likable about that person or admirable. That's one thing or you can ask questions about their background and so on. Oh, really. That's where I grew up. Or that's where I went to school in that area. Or that kind of thing. The commonality of place is remarkably powerful. People feel a sense of unity with with people who share common origins or common residence areas and so on. That sense of we becomes available to us then. But I think that's, that's the way to if you don't know anything about them. Spend a little time in sociable interaction to be able to identify some of those things and then simply point to them, mention them that you appreciate that about them. Yeah. Thank you. Yes, I mean, yes, and questions are always the key to learning. So yes, thank you. Yeah, that's a good question. Let's move on to Maria. Hi Dr Chalbini. Thank you so much for a great call. Oh my gosh, you just you're pouring gold on us here. I have like five pages of notes. My question is very really like field oriented. I am actually about to hopefully close the deal on doing the influence program in my area and we moved through two phases already to the final probably and I'm going to meet with the main decision financial institution in my area and the gatekeeper who kind of wasn't between mentioned to me that they have another consultant that they're considering. So I know there is a competition. And we've just learned a lot. Thank you by the way for the masterminds we brainstormed options. I have a plan and looking at my plan as I'm putting it strategically together. I'm just wondering that using the contrast principle. I can either come across as boastful or sounding to pushy when I bring this to their awareness. So what would you recommend to do in terms of balancing the contrast principle. This is my first question. Your question is how you are superior to this to this other consultant. Yeah, have them do it. That is what you would say to them at the outset is I'm so glad to be this far along in the in the process. And I want to ask, answer any questions you have of me. But I wonder if you could answer answer a question for me. What. What is it about me that has brought me to this level for you. Can I know that so I can help in install that to a greater extent or make sure that that's something that I have them tell you why they like you. And they will determine how you are superior to the other. They will search and come up with the reasons. That you are there. That you've gotten to this point. Right. And once again, with the principle of commitment and consistency will be they will be committing themselves to your favorable features. And you'll be publicly describing the things that are so desirable about your partnership with them. I know I have a colleague, not even a colleague it's acquaintance who says that when he goes for a job interview. He says, can you tell me why you invited me here. And he hears them describe all of the strengths that they value about him. Which gives him a chance to double down on those and show them how they were right about that. But it also gives them the experience of having of having praised you. You don't have to do it. They've done it for one another. And he says when it's a group, very often one person will say well I really like the way your experience that you have another person will say, well I like the way you were trained and another person will say, well I like the fit that you have with our values. So, now they're hearing it from one another. So, I think that's how I would recommend just asking them. Can you tell me what what it was about me that I can embellish going forward that you really think are important features of what I can. And you'll hear it. Thank you so much. And I have one more question that will help all of us here. You mentioned that you're continuously reading magazines case studies research. What resources would you recommend for us to sign up just so we can see how these principles really, you know, show up in daily life and references as social proof as we work with our clients say, different case studies like what resources. You're exactly right with this question you can't be expected to know which academic journals and which articles and what technology, you know, language they use in the articles that's jargon. That's why we started our newsletter. If you sign up for a newsletter. Every month you're going to get an update on what's new in the research environment relevant to the process of influence so that there's a way to describe it in language that people can understand who aren't trained as behavioral and that newsletter is going to roll out soon. That's exactly what Dr Chaldeany and boss have been working on it together and interpreting the academic level kind of jargon and digesting it down for you guys so I'm also looking forward to that coming out. I'm becoming very much interested in the relationship between persuasion and reflection. And give you a context like you said a powerful line now you said we have to inject personalism into the technology. And I'm thinking a lot about that and and part of it is well maybe also we have to inject thinking into personalism because AI is reducing our ability to think. So what do you have to say about that relationship between us reflecting on things that may be random and building that ability to create connection so that we can become better persuaded because persuasion seem always like we're persuading others. What about I don't know if that's the right technical term self persuasion. Yeah. And the way to do it it turns out there's a literature on this, primarily coming from a man named Richard petty at Ohio State University, asking rhetorical questions, questions that say, now, can you can you imagine how you would feel if this happened and you lost this or versus. Can you imagine how good you what, how would you feel with about this, where you ask people questions that they have to answer by going inside and making contact with their own values their own priorities their own preferences. You ask them those kinds of rhetorical questions, and they, they stimulate reflection. And when they hit on the things that you have to use us structured the question to ask them about, you know, well what are the, how would you feel if you were able to succeed in the ways that we are suggesting that you can succeed. What would you do as a, how would you feel what would you do with the, with the outcomes of that how would you spread the, the profits of those kinds of questions that asked them to reflect again on their own choices. The research is rhetorical questions increase persuasion. Thank you so much. Thank you. And I think for the reason you just I identified you self reflect. I'm your own priorities. Thank you so much. Yeah, yeah, I'm going to my region. Yeah. And the answer would be the four pillars that no program or book I know includes. The first is everything is based on scientific research. Yeah. Secondly, everything is based on an ethical approach to delivering that scientific research. Third, everything is based on applying that research. So, it's not just to learn about this. We do that too. Yeah, yeah, yeah. What point does that become self serving if you like it's all self serving to some extent. Why do you draw that line? Well, I think it is self serving but for the common good. That is, as long as what you are offering to learn more about is based on solid evidence that is ethically applied and gives them an advantage. It's mutual benefit. And I don't think we ever want to turn our back on mutual benefit, because one of those who benefits is us. Yeah, yeah, yeah. So I don't I don't have an objection to that. Okay.
